comment_text.sn4.4:1.2: The Buddha had come to Varanasi after his Awakening to teach the five ascetics (SN 56.11). This event is also preserved at Kd 1:13.1.1.
comment_text.sn4.4:2.1: “Rational application of mind and rational right effort” is yoniso manasikārā yoniso sammappadhānā. The term yoniso (“by way of womb”) indicates that the cause and effect are aligned.

root_text.sn4.4:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā bārāṇasiyaṁ viharati isipatane migadāye.
root_text.sn4.4:2.1: “Mayhaṁ kho, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā yoniso sammappadhānā anuttarā vimutti anuppattā, anuttarā vimutti sacchikatā.
root_text.sn4.4:2.2: Tumhepi, bhikkhave, yoniso manasikārā yoniso sammappadhānā anuttaraṁ vimuttiṁ anupāpuṇātha, anuttaraṁ vimuttiṁ sacchikarothā”ti.
root_text.sn4.4:2.3: Atha kho māro pāpimā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn4.4:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana.
translation_text.sn4.4:1.3: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn4.4:2.1: “Mendicants, I have attained and realized supreme freedom through rational application of mind and rational right effort.
translation_text.sn4.4:2.2: You too should attain and realize supreme freedom through rational application of mind and rational right effort.”
translation_text.sn4.4:2.3: Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse:

variant_text.sn4.4:4.1: Muttāhaṁ → muttohaṁ (bj, cck, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)

reference_text.sn4.4:1.1: bj13.192, ms12S1_783, msdiv140, pts-vp-pli1ed1.105, pts-vp-pli2ed1.235, sya15.153
